literal: fire; from PIE *h₁ngʷnis (cf. Latin *ignis*); in Sanskrit a masculine *i*-stem (*agniḥ*, nom. sg.) naming both the physical element fire and the deity who presides over it. The name is not derived from a verbal root *ag-* but is the inherited Indo-European word for fire.
invariant_definition: *Agni* is the Vedic fire-deity and the sacred fire kindled on the ritual altar, and at the same time — from the Ṛgveda onward — the god 'seated in the secret place' (*guhā niṣīdan*) of the heart, whose mantras are 'fashioned by the heart' (*hṛdā taṣṭān*). The figure sustains two valences that run in parallel across the textual tradition: (1) the ritual-cosmic fire, divine mediator between humans and gods, and (2) the interior flame, the immortal within the mortal, the deathless will-force or divine presence dwelling in the cave of the heart. In the Upaniṣads the word *agni* gradually yields to *jyotis* (light, flame) as the name for the inner self, but the figure — a smokeless, windless, self-luminous flame in the heart — persists. In the Bhagavad-Gītā this tradition of the interior flame becomes the background for two distinct images: the windless lamp that does *not* flicker (6.19), figuring the steadied yogin's mind, and the extinction-into-Brahman (*brahma-nirvāṇa*) where the flame of desire is blown out. The extinction-image of *nirvāṇa* (nir- + √vā, 'blown out') and the flame-image of Agni are therefore the same fire-figure at opposite poles of valence: *nirvāṇa* is the flame extinguished; Agni is the flame kindled and tended. The connection between them is resonance of imagery, not a single textual genealogy.

school_framing.shared_core: **Agni as the divine fire — cosmic mediator on the altar, immortal flame in the heart.** All textual layers share the fire-figure; they differ only in which aspect they foreground (ritual, cosmic, interior, psychological) and in the vocabulary they use (agni → jyotis → dīpa → nirvāṇa's blown-out flame).
school_framing.register_axes_note: The three interpretive layers below (Ṛgvedic, Upaniṣadic, Aurobindo) are not rival doctrinal schools contesting the same proposition; they are successive textual strata and one modern hermeneutic lens. **No real doctrinal disagreement** divides them on the fire-figure itself. The important note of difference is the valence: Aurobindo's reading foregrounds the flame as the divine will-force *kindled and tended* in man; the *nirvāṇa* image in the Gītā and Buddhist usage presupposes the same flame but inverts the valence — it is the flame *blown out*. These are not contradictory claims but opposite uses of the same underlying figure. Aurobindo's psychological reading is a hermeneutic lens, not a philological claim; it is marked as such below.

per_school.definition: In the Ṛgveda Agni is simultaneously the fire kindled on the sacrificial altar, the divine messenger (*dūta*) who carries offerings to the gods, and the god 'seated down in the secret place' (*guhā niṣīdan*, RV 1.67.3). The seers find him there; their mantras are described as 'fashioned by the heart' (*hṛdā taṣṭān*, RV 1.67.4). In hymns addressed to Agni Vaiśvānara — 'the fire common to all men' or 'the universal fire' — he is 'the immortal growing in body' (*amartyas tanvā vardhamānaḥ*, RV 6.9.4), the deathless within the mortal frame. The *guhā* in which Agni sits is at once the ritual hearth-pit and, in the tradition's own recurrent formula, a 'secret' or 'hidden' place that later interpretation identifies with the cave of the heart.
per_school.register_tag: Φ+R; Vedic ritual register — cosmic mediation and the interior secret place

per_school.definition: The Upaniṣads interiorise the Vedic fire-image. The inner self is now figured explicitly as a smokeless flame: 'the Person the measure of a thumb, like a light without smoke' (*jyotir iva adhūmakaḥ*, Kaṭha 2.1.13). The word *agni* steps back and *jyotis* (light, flame) steps forward, but the figure — a self-luminous, smokeless fire dwelling in the heart — is continuous with the Ṛgvedic hidden Agni in the *guhā*. The Bhagavad-Gītā will later extend both poles: the windless lamp (*dīpa*, 6.19) that does not flicker figures the stable yogin's mind, while the extinct flame of *nirvāṇa* figures the going-out of desire. These two uses — the undying inner flame and the extinguished flame — inherit the same Vedic-Upaniṣadic fire-figure at opposite valences.
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; Upaniṣadic register — interiorisation of the flame, jyotis as the inner self

per_school.definition: Aurobindo reads Agni in the Ṛgveda as 'the god in human beings, the immortal in mortals, the divine guest' and identifies him as 'the divine Will or Conscious-Power which is the Master' (Aurobindo, *The Secret of the Veda*, ch. 'Agni, the Divine Will-Force'). On this lens the guhā is the inner psychological depth of the human being, and the concealment-and-finding of Agni dramatises the soul's discovery of its own divine will-force. **This is Aurobindo's psychological-hermeneutic lens, not a philological claim.** The textual datum (Agni in the guhā, mantras heart-fashioned) is genuine; the identification of *guhā* with a psychological register, and of Agni with 'divine will-force', is an interpretive construction belonging to Aurobindo's late 19th–early 20th c. esoteric reading of the Veda.
primary_loci: Aurobindo, The Secret of the Veda, ch. 'Agni, the Divine Will-Force'
per_school.register_tag: Φ+I; interpretive lens — Aurobindo's psychological reading, NOT philological proof [INTERPRETIVE-LENS]

textual_history.summary: The career of Agni in the texts traces an arc from the altar and the hidden guhā of the Ṛgveda, through the interiorisation of the flame in the Upaniṣads, to the windless lamp and the extinct flame of the Bhagavad-Gītā. In the Ṛgveda (RV 1.67.3–4) Agni is simultaneously the cosmic fire and the god 'seated in the secret place' (*guhā*) whose mantras are heart-fashioned — the earliest pole of the imagery: a flame at once concealed and luminous in an interior place. The epithet Vaiśvānara ('common to all men') and the description 'the immortal growing in body' (RV 6.9.4) already give Agni a universal-interior character, the deathless within the mortal. In the Upaniṣads the word-figure shifts: *agni* yields to *jyotis*, and the inner flame becomes the explicit metaphor for the self — 'a light without smoke' in the heart (Kaṭha 2.1.13). The Bhagavad-Gītā inherits both ends of the figure: the steadied lamp that does *not* flicker (6.19, the positive inner flame) and the extinct flame of *nirvāṇa* (2.72, 5.24–26, 6.15, the going-out of desire). The connection between Agni kindled and *nirvāṇa*'s flame blown out is **resonance of imagery, not a documented textual genealogy**: the Gītā nowhere cites the heart-fire of RV 1.67 when it speaks of *brahma-nirvāṇa*, and the immediate counterpart for its extinction-language is Buddhist *nibbāna*. What the Vedic and Upaniṣadic Agni-figure supplies is the intelligibility of the interior fire that can, at one extreme, be tended and kindled (Agni), and at the other, blown out (*nirvāṇa*).

stages.sanskrit: haste dadhāno nṛmṇā viśvāny ame devān dhād guhā niṣīdan
stages.rendering: Holding in his hand all manly powers, he set the gods [in their place] as he sat down in the secret place (guhā).
stages.note: Agni holds all divine powers and is found 'seated in the guhā' — the hidden or secret place that the tradition reads as the cave of the heart. This is the oldest textual pole of the imagery cluster: the sacred fire is at once manifest on the altar and concealed in an interior secret.

stages.sanskrit: vidantīm atra naro dhiyaṁdhā hṛdā yat taṣṭān mantrām̐ aśaṁsan
stages.rendering: There the men holding the thought found him — when, fashioned by the heart, they spoke forth the mantras.
stages.note: The seers find Agni in the secret place and their mantras are 'hṛdā taṣṭān' — fashioned or carved by the heart. The interior dimension of the fire is explicit in the Ṛgveda itself; it is not a late Upaniṣadic innovation. The anusvara characters (ṁ) are preserved verbatim from the Aufrecht edition as supplied.

stages.sanskrit: ayaṁ sa jajñe dhruva ā niṣatto 'martyas tanvā3 vardhamānaḥ
stages.rendering: He is born, the firm one, seated here — the immortal, growing in body.
stages.note: Agni Vaiśvānara ('the fire common to all' or 'the universal fire in man') is explicitly called *amartyaḥ* — the immortal. This is the 'immortal in mortals' reading that Aurobindo foregrounds; the text supports the attribution of deathlessness to the fire-within-the-mortal, even if Aurobindo's further identification with 'divine will-force' is his own interpretive step. The *tanvā3* with the pluta (lengthened) vowel is preserved as in the Aufrecht edition.

stages.rendering: The Person the measure of a thumb, like a light without smoke.
stages.note: The Vedic hidden Agni is interiorised as the *puruṣa* in the heart, and the vocabulary shifts: the word is now *jyotis* (light, flame), not *agni*, but the figure is a smokeless flame — precisely what a well-fed, wind-sheltered sacred fire is. Flag: the fire-figure persists; the word has shifted. The 'light without smoke' (*adhūmakaḥ*) is the interior self, deathless, dwelling in the heart. This is the direct ancestor of the Gītā's windless lamp.

stages.sanskrit: yathā dīpo nivāta-stho neṅgate sopamā smṛtā
stages.rendering: As a lamp in a windless place does not flicker — that is the simile remembered.
stages.note: The only place the Gītā makes the flame-figure explicit for the yogin's steadied mind. The lamp (*dīpa*) that does NOT flicker is the positive pole: the inner fire tended and stilled, not extinguished. Contrast with the *nirvāṇa* image in the same text (2.72, 5.24–26): there the flame of desire is blown OUT. Same fire-figure; opposite valence. The connection between Agni kindled in the guhā (RV) and the lamp not blown out (Gītā 6.19) is continuous; the connection to *nirvāṇa*'s blown-out flame is resonance of imagery inverted, not a genealogical citation.

translator_note: Do not flatten the three senses of *agni* into one. (1) Agni the deity: a divine person, the cosmic fire-god, first-born mediator between mortals and immortals — not 'fire' in a generic sense. (2) Fire the element: the physical sacrificial flame on the altar, which is also the vehicle for Agni the person. (3) The inner flame: the hidden fire in the cave of the heart, the immortal within the mortal, the smokeless self-luminous presence — a reading that begins in the Ṛgveda (*guhā niṣīdan*, RV 1.67.3) and is made explicit as a trope in the Upaniṣads (*jyotir iva adhūmakaḥ*, Kaṭha 2.1.13). The heart-fire reading is interpretive in the sense that it requires taking the *guhā* of the Ṛgvedic Agni-hymns as the interior of the human person, not merely a ritual pit or cosmic hiding-place; the tradition itself (including the Upaniṣads) performs this interiorisation, so it is not external allegory but an internal textual development. Aurobindo's further step — identifying the inner flame with a 'divine will-force' or 'conscious-power' — is his own psychological hermeneutic; it is well-grounded in the imagery but is not a neutral philological reading and should be attributed to him. Finally: *nirvāṇa*'s flame (nir- + √vā, 'blown out') and Agni's flame are the same figure at opposite poles — kindled vs extinguished. The resonance is real and illuminating; the genealogy is not documented. Do not claim a textual citation where only image-resonance exists.

cognates.note: One of the most secure Indo-European etymologies: Skt. agní, Latin ignis, Lith. ugnìs and OCS ognь all continue PIE *h₁n̥gʷnis, ‘fire’ conceived as an active, animate power — distinct from the neuter *péh₂ur ‘fire as substance’ (Greek pŷr, English fire). English igneous and ignite are later borrowings from Latin ignis.
